# PT Study System (Trey)

Owner: Trey.

Personal study OS that captures sessions, produces metrics and Anki-ready outputs, keeps Obsidian as the primary knowledge base, and improves over time through Scholar research.

Response style: straight to the point, no fluff.

## User-Directed Collaboration

- Default to user intent first. Start from the user's stated objective and preferred operating model.
- Find the best way to accomplish what the user wants in the way they want it done.
- Present tradeoffs, risks, and alternatives when relevant, but do not substitute the agent's own preference for the user's stated intent.
- Do not assume the user is unaware of the tradeoffs they are choosing; only push for clarification when requirements are ambiguous, contradictory, or materially risky.

### First-Response Pattern

- Lead with how to make the user's requested outcome work.
- Then add pros, cons, constraints, or safer variants as decision support.
- Do not reframe the task around what the agent would prefer the user to want.

Example:

```text
User: "I want full access to these tools."
Preferred response shape: "Here is the best way to give you full access in one setup. The main tradeoffs are X and Y. If you still want full power, this is the cleanest implementation."
```

## Critical Guardrails

- Destructive async UI flows must disable repeat actions, normalize optional payloads, and centralize close/reset behavior.
- Let shared dialog primitives handle centering. Do not hardcode modal `top/left/transform` positioning.
- If backend behavior looks unchanged after a fix, verify which PID is actually listening on port `5000` before trusting the result.
- Tutor scoped retrieval must carry explicit `content_filter.material_ids` and `accuracy_profile` when selected-material scope matters.
- Session delete should preserve best-effort Obsidian cleanup behavior plus persistent delete telemetry.

## JavaScript REPL (Node)
- Use `js_repl` for Node-backed JavaScript with top-level await in a persistent kernel.
- `js_repl` is a freeform/custom tool. Direct `js_repl` calls must send raw JavaScript tool input (optionally with first-line `// codex-js-repl: timeout_ms=15000`). Do not wrap code in JSON (for example `{"code":"..."}`), quotes, or markdown code fences.
- Helpers: `codex.tmpDir`, `codex.tool(name, args?)`, and `codex.emitImage(imageLike)`.
- `codex.tool` executes a normal tool call and resolves to the raw tool output object. Use it for shell and non-shell tools alike. Nested tool outputs stay inside JavaScript unless you emit them explicitly.
- `codex.emitImage(...)` adds one image to the outer `js_repl` function output each time you call it, so you can call it multiple times to emit multiple images. It accepts a data URL, a single `input_image` item, an object like `{ bytes, mimeType }`, or a raw tool response object with exactly one image and no text. It rejects mixed text-and-image content.
- Example of sharing an in-memory Playwright screenshot: `await codex.emitImage({ bytes: await page.screenshot({ type: "jpeg", quality: 85 }), mimeType: "image/jpeg" })`.
- Example of sharing a local image tool result: `await codex.emitImage(codex.tool("view_image", { path: "/absolute/path" }))`.
- When encoding an image to send with `codex.emitImage(...)` or `view_image`, prefer JPEG at about 85 quality when lossy compression is acceptable; use PNG when transparency or lossless detail matters. Smaller uploads are faster and less likely to hit size limits.
- Top-level bindings persist across cells. If a cell throws, prior bindings remain available and bindings that finished initializing before the throw often remain usable in later cells. For code you plan to reuse across cells, prefer declaring or assigning it in direct top-level statements before operations that might throw. If you hit `SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared`, reuse the binding, pick a new name, wrap in `{ ... }` for block scope, or reset the kernel with `js_repl_reset`.
- Top-level static import declarations (for example `import x from "./file.js"`) are currently unsupported in `js_repl`; use dynamic imports with `await import("pkg")`, `await import("./file.js")`, or `await import("/abs/path/file.mjs")` instead. Imported local files must be ESM `.js`/`.mjs` files and run in the same REPL VM context. Bare package imports always resolve from REPL-global search roots (`CODEX_JS_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, then cwd), not relative to the imported file location. Local files may statically import only other local relative/absolute/`file://` `.js`/`.mjs` files; package and builtin imports from local files must stay dynamic. `import.meta.resolve()` returns importable strings such as `file://...`, bare package names, and `node:...` specifiers. Local file modules reload between execs, while top-level bindings persist until `js_repl_reset`.
- Avoid direct access to `process.stdout` / `process.stderr` / `process.stdin`; it can corrupt the JSON line protocol. Use `console.log`, `codex.tool(...)`, and `codex.emitImage(...)`.

I was working with Grok to help me clear up some ideas and here is a prompt he wrote. Just reply for now. You are an expert study-system architect helping me build a single, unified web dashboard (built on top of my Obsidian vault) for all my learning.
My complete vision:
I have an Obsidian vault that is my single source of truth. An LLM is already hooked into Obsidian to keep all notes formatted and organized.
My main dashboard (running on my Lenovo Yoga Book 9i with dual touchscreens and Apple Pencil) has several dedicated pages/tabs that work together seamlessly:

Preprocessing Studio: A focused page where I select one file or a batch of files (notes, PDFs, etc.). The LLM helps me extract measurable learning objectives, build hierarchical outlines, flowcharts, and graphs. It generates Mermaid mind maps, editable tables, and visual summaries — all saved directly back into Obsidian as clean, linked Markdown files.
Media + Notes Studio: I can drag in or open any file (PDFs, PowerPoints, Word docs, MP4 videos). The file opens in a clean viewer on one screen while an Excalidraw canvas (or Obsidian canvas) sits side-by-side on the second screen so I can take stylus notes in real time. Everything auto-saves back to Obsidian (linked to the original file and any timestamps for videos).
Visualization Hub: A gallery/preview area where I can view, edit, and drag-and-drop all generated mind maps, graphs, drawings, and tables.
Anki Flashcard Manager: Built-in tools to auto-generate cloze or Q&A cards directly from preprocessed material and add them to my decks.
RAG Explorer Chat: A quick side chat that can search across my entire embedded library and Obsidian vault for connections or extra context.
Tutor Page: Once preprocessing is done, I load one or more clean bundles. The tutor skips all setup steps and goes straight into teaching, explaining, quizzing, and guiding Anki reviews across multiple files.

Additional nice-to-have: Sync with Obsidian on my iPad so I can handwrite notes on the iPad while media plays on the Yoga Book (notes appear instantly in the main vault).
Overall workflow I want:

Raw material → Preprocessing Studio (or Media + Notes Studio) → everything organized with objectives, hierarchies, visuals, tables, and Anki cards already created and saved in Obsidian.
Then switch to Tutor page for actual studying — no more front-end hassle, no manual copy-paste, no waiting for the tutor to re-analyze everything.

I already have:

Excalidraw fully working
Mermaid mind map generation
Canvas/drawing pad with Apple Pencil
Table builder
Anki integration on the site
RAG system with embedded files
Obsidian LLM formatting hook
Basic file open for some media types

Now, based on everything I just described, please analyze my current setup and answer these exact questions:

What parts of this vision do I already have fully implemented?
What parts are partially built or working but need polishing?
What are the biggest gaps I still need to fill to make the full seamless workflow real?
List the gaps in order of easiest-to-hardest to implement.

Be precise and only reference the tools and pages I mentioned above. Do not suggest agents, MCP, multi-model orchestration, or any external frameworks.

# PT Study System (Trey)

Owner: Trey.

Personal study OS that captures sessions, produces metrics and Anki-ready outputs, keeps Obsidian as the primary knowledge base, and improves over time through Scholar research.

Response style: straight to the point, no fluff.

## User-Directed Collaboration

- Default to user intent first. Start from the user's stated objective and preferred operating model.
- Find the best way to accomplish what the user wants in the way they want it done.
- Present tradeoffs, risks, and alternatives when relevant, but do not substitute the agent's own preference for the user's stated intent.
- Do not assume the user is unaware of the tradeoffs they are choosing; only push for clarification when requirements are ambiguous, contradictory, or materially risky.

### First-Response Pattern

- Lead with how to make the user's requested outcome work.
- Then add pros, cons, constraints, or safer variants as decision support.
- Do not reframe the task around what the agent would prefer the user to want.

Example:

```text
User: "I want full access to these tools."
Preferred response shape: "Here is the best way to give you full access in one setup. The main tradeoffs are X and Y. If you still want full power, this is the cleanest implementation."
```

## JavaScript REPL (Node)
- Use `js_repl` for Node-backed JavaScript with top-level await in a persistent kernel.
- `js_repl` is a freeform/custom tool. Direct `js_repl` calls must send raw JavaScript tool input (optionally with first-line `// codex-js-repl: timeout_ms=15000`). Do not wrap code in JSON (for example `{"code":"..."}`), quotes, or markdown code fences.
- Helpers: `codex.tmpDir`, `codex.tool(name, args?)`, and `codex.emitImage(imageLike)`.
- `codex.tool` executes a normal tool call and resolves to the raw tool output object. Use it for shell and non-shell tools alike. Nested tool outputs stay inside JavaScript unless you emit them explicitly.
- `codex.emitImage(...)` adds one image to the outer `js_repl` function output each time you call it, so you can call it multiple times to emit multiple images. It accepts a data URL, a single `input_image` item, an object like `{ bytes, mimeType }`, or a raw tool response object with exactly one image and no text. It rejects mixed text-and-image content.
- Example of sharing an in-memory Playwright screenshot: `await codex.emitImage({ bytes: await page.screenshot({ type: "jpeg", quality: 85 }), mimeType: "image/jpeg" })`.
- Example of sharing a local image tool result: `await codex.emitImage(codex.tool("view_image", { path: "/absolute/path" }))`.
- When encoding an image to send with `codex.emitImage(...)` or `view_image`, prefer JPEG at about 85 quality when lossy compression is acceptable; use PNG when transparency or lossless detail matters. Smaller uploads are faster and less likely to hit size limits.
- Top-level bindings persist across cells. If a cell throws, prior bindings remain available and bindings that finished initializing before the throw often remain usable in later cells. For code you plan to reuse across cells, prefer declaring or assigning it in direct top-level statements before operations that might throw. If you hit `SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared`, reuse the binding, pick a new name, wrap in `{ ... }` for block scope, or reset the kernel with `js_repl_reset`.
- Top-level static import declarations (for example `import x from "./file.js"`) are currently unsupported in `js_repl`; use dynamic imports with `await import("pkg")`, `await import("./file.js")`, or `await import("/abs/path/file.mjs")` instead. Imported local files must be ESM `.js`/`.mjs` files and run in the same REPL VM context. Bare package imports always resolve from REPL-global search roots (`CODEX_JS_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, then cwd), not relative to the imported file location. Local files may statically import only other local relative/absolute/`file://` `.js`/`.mjs` files; package and builtin imports from local files must stay dynamic. `import.meta.resolve()` returns importable strings such as `file://...`, bare package names, and `node:...` specifiers. Local file modules reload between execs, while top-level bindings persist until `js_repl_reset`.
- Avoid direct access to `process.stdout` / `process.stderr` / `process.stdin`; it can corrupt the JSON line protocol. Use `console.log`, `codex.tool(...)`, and `codex.emitImage(...)`.

Text:
Best way to begin is `inventory first, redesign second`.

If we try to design the Studio before we know exactly what tools, buttons, and flows already exist, we’ll just recreate drift. So I’d organize this in five passes:

1. `Tool inventory`
   - list every implemented tool/action/button we already have
   - group by what it actually does, not by current page name

2. `Studio buckets`
   - sort that inventory into:
   - `View` = PDF, Word, MP4, file open, source browsing
   - `Create` = notes, canvas, graph, table, cards
   - `Organize` = board, bundles, objectives, outlines, sorting
   - `Publish` = save to vault, push to Anki, finalize artifacts
   - `Tutor handoff` = what enters Tutor and what comes back out

3. `Studio flow design`
   - define the actual flow:
   - open material
   - inspect/view
   - extract
   - build artifacts
   - organize on board
   - finalize/publish
   - launch Tutor or send Tutor outputs back into Studio

4. `Studio chain`
   - pull only the methods/chains that help with pre-tutor prep:
   - objective extraction
   - hierarchy building
   - map/table generation
   - source-grounded questioning
   - bundle prep
   - leave deep teaching, retrieval, and main note synthesis inside Tutor

5. `Gap audit`
   - check what’s missing for the Studio vision:
   - real document/video viewers
   - board workflow
   - artifact publishing flow
   - Tutor <-> Studio transfer rules
   - whether current graph/table/canvas tools are actually the best surfaces

So the first concrete inventory should be in this order:

- `Sources / Library / file-open tools`
- `Workspace tools`
- `Artifact generation tools`
- `Save / publish / export actions`
- `Tutor-only actions that might move to Studio`

Recommended next step: I do a short `current tool inventory` grouped into those five buckets, and we use that as the base for the Studio design.

**1. Intake And Source Access**
Implemented:
- `Library upload`: PDF, DOCX, PPTX, MD, TXT, MP4
- `Library folder sync`: scan folder tree, choose files, sync selected files only
- `Course linking`: link uploaded/synced materials to a course
- `Library organization`: folder rail, course rail, unlinked view

- that is not yet a real `native PDF / Word / MP4 viewer`

**2. Studio-Capable Creation Tools**
Implemented:
- `Notes`: Tutor workspace note editor, create new note, open pinned vault notes, save to vault, wikilink open
- `Canvas`: Excalidraw with save, load, export PNG, fullscreen, templates
- `Graph`: graph panel / unified graph surface
- `Table`: add/remove rows and columns, mark key row, copy markdown, export PNG, save to vault
- `Artifacts from Tutor`: note, card, map, structured notes
- `Map handling`: Mermaid map preview, save as Obsidian canvas, open linked Figma URL if present
- `Anki tools`: draft review, approve, edit, delete, sync cards, status checking

This is the strongest current Studio foundation:
- `notes`
- `canvas`
- `graph`
- `table`
- `flashcards`

**3. Tutor-Only Study Controls**
Implemented:
- `Wizard`: course, study unit, objective scope, focus objective, topic/session label, materials, chain mode, template/custom/auto, recent session resume/delete, start session
- `Chat controls`: sources toggle, accuracy profile, behavior overrides (`Socratic`, `Evaluate`, `Concept Map`, `Teach-Back`)
- `Retrieval/source toggles`: materials, Obsidian, Gemini Vision, web, deep think
- `Session controls`: workspace toggle, artifacts toggle, export, end session, ship to brain
- `Chain controls`: block timer, pause timer, skip/advance block
- `Scholar strategy`: visible strategy card plus learner feedback controls

This confirms Tutor currently owns both:
- `study execution`
- too much `prep/setup`

**4. Partial Or Structurally Off**
Partial:
- `Library preview` exists, but not true side-by-side document/media viewing
- `MP4 pipeline` exists for processing/enrichment, but not as a clean study viewer surface
- `Vault tools` exist inside Tutor Sources, but they feel like utility controls, not a real studio workspace
- `Artifacts` exist, but not yet as a unified gallery / staging / publish board
- `Workspace` exists, but there is no dedicated `Studio shell` around it

Not seeing as a real implemented surface yet:
- `native PDF viewer`
- `native DOCX/Word viewer`
- `native MP4 player/viewer`
- `drag/sort board for images, ideas, and artifacts`
- `single Studio route` that unifies prep, creation, organizing, and publishing

**5. Main Takeaway**
You already have a lot of the `tooling`.
The bigger issue is that it’s split across:
- `Library`
- `Tutor Wizard`
- `Tutor Sources`
- `Tutor Workspace`
- `Tutor Artifacts`

So the Studio build looks more like:
- `reorganize existing tools first`
- then add the missing `viewer + board + publishing` surfaces

Recommended next step: I take this inventory and do a `Studio grouping pass`:
- `Keep in Tutor`
- `Move to Studio`
- `Shared between Tutor and Studio`
- `Missing entirely`

That should make the redesign much cleaner.

Text:
Here’s the first clean cut.

**Matrix**
| Bucket | Exact Tutor controls/features | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| `Stay in Tutor` | chat input, `Send` / `Stop`, accuracy profile (`Balanced/Strict/Coverage`), behavior overrides (`Socratic`, `Evaluate`, `Concept Map`, `Teach-Back`), source toggles (`Materials`, `Obsidian`, `Gemini Vision`, `Web`, `Deep Think`), per-message actions (`Save Note`, `Create Card`, `Create Map`, `Save Table`, `Save Map`), verdict badges, teach-back badges, chain block timer, `Pause/Resume`, `Skip`, `END`, `SHIP TO BRAIN`, scholar strategy card | This is the actual study loop: explain, quiz, retrieve, judge, advance |
| `Move to Studio` | most of Wizard setup (`Course`, `Study Unit`, `Focus Objective`, session label, material picking, chain selection/building), material upload/manage (`Process MP4`, `Enrich`, move, delete), vault utility work (`NEW FOLDER`, `NEW NOTE`, `EDIT`, `RENAME`, `DELETE`), flashcard batch management (`Approve`, `Delete`, `Edit`, `Sync Cards`), pre-study objective prep, publish/finalize flows | This is prep, library management, staging, and publishing work |
| `Shared Between Tutor And Studio` | `NOTES`, `CANVAS`, `GRAPH`, `TABLE`, artifact gallery, save-to-vault actions, canvas templates/save/load/export, graph editing, table editing, quick pinned note open, artifact review | These are work surfaces, not page identities |
| `Missing Entirely` | native PDF viewer, native DOCX/Word viewer, native MP4 player, timestamped video notes, drag/sort board for excerpts/images/ideas, unified Studio route/shell, prepared-bundle handoff into Tutor, publish queue that shows `vault / anki / brain` destinations together | These are the real Studio gaps |

My read: the biggest structural problem is not “missing note tools.” It’s that the app already has most of the creation surfaces, but they’re trapped inside Tutor instead of being reorganized around a Studio.

**What Good Study Studios Tend To Do**
The best products in this space keep the workflow very close to:

`source visible -> excerpt/select -> synthesize -> organize visually -> publish/review`

Patterns that showed up repeatedly:
- side-by-side reading and note-making
- easy excerpt linking back to source context
- visual organization across notes, maps, and cards
- low-friction handwriting/stylus support
- multi-device sync / multi-window workflows

That pattern shows up in [LiquidText](https://www.liquidtext.net/), [Flexcil](https://www.flexcil.com/), and MarginNote’s public descriptions via the [App Store listing](https://apps.apple.com/rs/app/marginnote-4/id1531657269) and [user guide](https://manual.marginnote.cn/en/study/).

**Tool Direction**
What looks right for your stack:
- Keep `Excalidraw` for freeform thinking, stylus work, boards, image arrangement.
- Keep `@xyflow/react` for structured concept maps and editable relationship graphs.
- Keep `Mermaid` as the LLM generation format, then let users convert/edit in the richer surfaces.
- Add `PDF.js` for the embedded PDF viewer. It’s a good fit for web-native reading and rendering. [PDF.js](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/)
- Add an MP4 viewer with timestamp hooks. Native HTML5 video may be enough; `Video.js` is the heavier option if you want richer controls. [Video.js](https://videojs.com/)
- For Word docs, a text-first preview layer like [Mammoth.js](https://github.com/mwilliamson/mammoth.js) is plausible, but DOCX is the weakest of the three viewer categories and may need a fidelity tradeoff.

Repo reality check:
- current frontend already has `@excalidraw/excalidraw`, `@xyflow/react`, and Mermaid-related support
- I did not find existing PDF/DOCX/video viewer dependencies in `dashboard_rebuild/package.json`

**What The Research Points Toward**
The strongest recurring study moves are:
- retrieval practice over passive rereading
- spaced review over cramming
- self-explanation and source-grounded explanation
- concept maps/drawings as synthesis tools, not as a substitute for retrieval

Useful evidence:
- Dunlosky’s review still strongly supports practice testing and distributed practice as high-utility techniques. [Strengthening the Student Toolbox](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1529100612453266)
- Roediger and Butler review retrieval practice as a strong long-term retention tool. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20951630/)
- Karpicke and Blunt found retrieval practice outperformed elaborative study with concept mapping for later learning outcomes. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21252317/)
- There’s also physiology-specific evidence that retrieval practice can improve evaluation of complex material, which matters for PT content. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29388259/)
- Self-explanation helps, but it works best when it is active and targeted, not just decorative prompting. [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25626750/) and [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9769186/)

The practical implication for your Studio:
- don’t optimize for “make lots of artifacts”
- optimize for `view source -> ask good questions -> extract objectives -> build a map/table/note -> test yourself -> push only the useful outputs`

Recommended next step: define the `Studio shell` itself before we get lost in controls.

I’d break the Studio into 5 zones:
1. `Source Viewer`
2. `Workbench` (`Notes / Canvas / Graph / Table`)
3. `Board` for sorting excerpts, images, ideas
4. `Publish Queue` (`Vault / Anki / Brain`)
5. `Tutor Handoff` (`Start Tutor from this bundle`)

If you want, next I can sketch that Studio shell in the same concrete way:
- top bar
- left rail
- center panes
- right rail
- exact buttons/actions for each area

The practical implication for your Studio:
- don’t optimize for “make lots of artifacts”
- optimize for `view source -> ask good questions -> extract objectives -> build a map/table/note -> test yourself -> push only the useful outputs`

Yes. The cleanest way to explain what you’re visualizing is this:

`The app is not a set of separate pages anymore. It’s a project-based study workspace.`

A `project` is your active class/topic container.
- It holds the materials you’re reading
- the notes and boards you’re building
- the maps, tables, and cards you generate
- the Tutor context and session history
- the timer and working state

Once you open a project, the app should stay inside that project’s context.
- if you switch between `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Library`, or `Notes`, nothing reloads from scratch
- the selected materials, active notes, board state, and Tutor context stay attached to that project
- you are moving between modes of the same project, not starting over on different pages

So the high-level model becomes:

1. `Project Dashboard`
- start new project
- open recent project
- open saved/archived project
- maybe use templates or workflows later

2. `Project Workspace`
- `Studio mode` for reading, viewing, sorting, outlining, mapping, note prep, dictation, timer
- `Tutor mode` for teaching, explanation, quizzing, retrieval, and main sense-making notes
- both modes share the same project memory and artifacts

3. `Publish Layer`
- project outputs stay in the project while they are still messy or in progress
- only finalized notes/cards/maps get pushed to the master Obsidian vault / Anki / brain surfaces
- this gives you a staging area before promotion

- `project working memory` = temporary, active, editable, contextual
- `master Obsidian / master files` = published, organized, durable source of truth

That also explains your material-transfer concern:
- materials belong to the project while you work
- outputs can stay project-local until finalized
- publishing is a deliberate promotion step, not automatic dumping

So if I were to phrase your vision in one sentence:

`I want a persistent project-based study OS where Studio and Tutor are two modes inside the same live project, and finalized outputs get published from the project into my master Obsidian system.`

That feels like the right big picture.

Next thing to define is the boundary between:
- what stays `inside the project`
- what gets `published to master vault`

That’s probably the most important architectural decision now.

The model now looks like this:

- `Master`
  - your real Obsidian system
  - final, published, organized knowledge

- `Project`
  - class/topic container, like `Anatomy`
  - holds the long-running context: materials, objectives, important notes, Tutor history, boards, and project-level outputs

- `Session`
  - a focused study run inside the project, like `Week 4 Upper Limb` or `Brachial Plexus PPT`
  - holds the temporary working state for that one study session

So a session becomes your `virtual study room`.

Inside a session you would keep:
- the files selected for that session
- the active note or scratch note
- board/canvas state
- maps/tables/cards generated during that run
- timer state
- Tutor conversation and artifacts
- voice dictation/transcript snippets if we add them

Then the promotion flow is:

- `Session -> Project`
  - promote the good notes/artifacts from that one session into the broader class/topic project

- `Project -> Master`
  - publish the truly final versions into Obsidian / Anki / whatever becomes the permanent system

That gives you three important benefits:

- `less clutter`
  - every study run does not dump half-finished notes straight into the master vault

- `better continuity`
  - once you’re inside `Anatomy`, the app keeps the Anatomy context alive across Studio and Tutor

- `clean session history`
  - you can study one week, one lecture, one powerpoint, one lab topic at a time without losing the broader project structure

- `Session`: Week 5 Thorax Lecture
- `Session`: Week 5 Thorax Lab Review
- `Session`: Brachial Plexus PowerPoint
- each session can produce notes/maps/cards
- some get promoted to the `Anatomy project`
- only the best/final ones get published to `Master Obsidian`

So the mental model is no longer:
- “open a page and do work”

It becomes:
- “open a project”
- “start or resume a session inside it”
- “work in Studio or Tutor without losing context”
- “promote outputs upward when ready”

That is much clearer than the current page-based flow.

The next thing we should pin down is the difference between:
- `project artifacts`
- `session artifacts`

Because that will determine what the dashboard and sidebars actually show.

Text:
That makes sense, and it’s a better use of the Studio.

The clean split becomes:

- `Tutor`
  - live studying
  - explaining
  - quizzing
  - rough sense-making
  - catching good ideas in the moment

- `Studio`
  - the cleanup room
  - collect what came out of Tutor
  - organize it
  - summarize it
  - turn it into usable notes/cards/maps
  - decide what gets promoted to project or master

So Studio is less “pre-study only” and more `post-session processing + organization`, with some prep capabilities too.

The most important new concept here is probably a `Studio Inbox`.

Inside a session, Tutor can send things into the Studio Inbox in two ways:

- `Send to Studio`
  - preserve the item as-is
  - exact wording, exact idea, no summarization
  - good for moments where the response is already strong

- `Compact to Studio`
  - summarize the important points from a message, exchange, or chunk of the session
  - good for token pressure and for end-of-session cleanup
  - should pull out:
    - key ideas
    - definitions
    - examples
    - open questions
    - maybe candidate cards or note bullets

That gives you two different capture modes:
- `raw capture`
- `processed capture`

And it fits what you said about compaction:
- if the session is getting long, the system can compact important material into Studio without losing it
- then Studio becomes the place where those compactions are reviewed and turned into final notes

So the hierarchy now feels like:

- `Master`
- `Project`
- `Session`
- `Studio Inbox` as the staging layer between session work and cleaned-up outputs

I’d phrase the workflow like this:

1. study in Tutor
2. send strong items to Studio as-is
3. compact longer segments to Studio when needed
4. open Studio after studying
5. clean, organize, merge, summarize
6. promote to project
7. publish final material to master Obsidian / Anki

This also gives us a much better replacement for the vague word `artifact`.
We can use:
- `Inbox`
- `Captures`
- `Drafts`
- `Session outputs`

`Studio Inbox` is probably the clearest.

One thing I would avoid is making Studio just a blind junk pile forever.
Better model:
- `Inbox` = unsorted dump
- `Workbench` = where you clean and merge things
- `Publish` = where finalized outputs go out

The next decision is the shape of that Tutor button under chat:
- one button with a small menu
- or two separate buttons visible all the time

My instinct is:
- `To Studio`
- dropdown/menu: `Raw` and `Compact`

That keeps the UI cleaner than two full buttons under every message.

**Current Snapshot**

We moved from “audit the Tutor page” into a clearer product model for the whole study workflow.

The big shift is this:
- the app should feel `project-based`, not just `page-based`
- `Tutor` should stay the main place for understanding and sense-making
- `Studio` should become the place where material is prepared, collected, cleaned up, organized, and promoted
- final outputs should not dump straight into the master vault while they are still messy

No files were created from this planning work. We stayed in discussion, repo inspection, UI inspection, tests, and research.

**What We’ve Locked In**

1. `Brain`
- Brain loading first is acceptable.
- The dashboard/home look feels weak and should be redesigned.
- This is on the change list.

2. `Tutor should not own all setup`
- Right now Tutor starts in Wizard/setup mode.
- That feels like the wrong default.
- Tutor currently owns too much front-end prep friction.

3. `Tutor remains the main sense-making surface`
- You do want some note-taking and pre-work outside Tutor.
- But the main understanding and the most important final study notes should still come from Tutor.
- We are not moving “real studying” out of Tutor.

4. `Studio is the cleanup and organization room`
- Studio is not just pre-study.
- Studio is where you come back after studying to:
  - clean up notes
  - organize outputs
  - summarize what matters
  - turn rough work into usable study material
  - decide what gets promoted upward

5. `Project-based structure`
The current best model is:

- `Master`
  - permanent Obsidian / Anki / long-term knowledge system
- `Project`
  - class/topic container, like `Anatomy`
- `Session`
  - focused study run inside a project, like `Week 4 Thorax PPT`
- `Studio Inbox / Workbench`
  - staging area for things coming out of Tutor before they are cleaned up and promoted

6. `Session model`

- `Project -> Master`
  - publish finalized outputs to Obsidian / Anki / master system

- explanation
- questioning
- retrieval
- sense-making

4. `Studio`
- inbox of captured items
- summary board / workbench
- cleanup, merge, sort, polish
- staging area before project/master promotion

- retrieval and behavior controls

- publishing workflows

- notes

Best ideas to borrow: `parallel reading workspace`, `drag excerpt to workspace`, `tap note -> jump back to source`, `cross-document linking`.

- [Flexcil](https://www.flexcil.com/): strongest pattern is `PDF and note-taking at the same time` with low-friction pen gestures. The official site leans hard on reading plus handwritten notes in one flow, with cross-document reference links. Best ideas to borrow: `viewer + notebook side by side`, `gesture-first markup`, `lightweight reference links`, `floating note overlays or quick note pane`.

- [MarginNote 4](https://apps.apple.com/la/app/marginnote-4-ai-notes-mindmap/id1531657269): strongest pattern is a `study set / project` container. One project can hold PDFs, EPUBs, videos, and audio, then layer on mind maps, concept diagrams, boards, backlinks, flashcards, timestamps, split view, and external-monitor support. Best ideas to borrow: `project container`, `dual-article view`, `card board`, `mind map + outliner + flashcards`, `timestamp/source backlinks`, `multi-window external-monitor support`.

- [Heptabase](https://www.goedel.io/p/unleashing-the-mind-heptabase): strongest pattern is `one global card system feeding multiple views`. The review highlights a 3-column layout, PDF highlights becoming reusable cards, and maps/whiteboards where those cards can be arranged, linked, and reused across contexts. Best ideas to borrow: `cards as reusable atomic units`, `right-sidebar highlight browser`, `drop cards onto maps`, `same content visible in library/map/task views`, `3-column desktop layout`.

- [LiquidText home / deeper dive](https://www.liquidtext.net/): confirms a few details especially relevant to your use case: import/export of working files, touch/pen support, and multi-monitor workflows. That reinforces the idea that your app should treat `source viewing + workspace manipulation` as the center of the experience, not chat alone.

- Top workflow pattern across all of them: `open source -> highlight/excerpt -> send into working area -> connect/group -> summarize/convert into notes/cards -> revisit source from any output`. That is the clearest external validation for your `Studio Inbox / Summary Board` direction.

- Top page-layout pattern worth borrowing: `left navigation / center source / right working context`, with the working context changing between `notes`, `board`, `map`, or `outline`. Heptabase’s 3-column structure and LiquidText’s parallel workspace both point in that direction.

- Top multi-screen pattern worth borrowing: `keep the source viewer detachable or pop-out capable`. LiquidText explicitly supports multi-monitor, and MarginNote’s listing explicitly mentions multi-window and external-monitor support. For your setup, that argues for `Studio main shell on one screen + viewer or notes popped out to the other`.

- Top board pattern worth borrowing: don’t make the board a canvas of anonymous shapes. MarginNote’s `Card Board` and Heptabase’s `card-based maps` both suggest the better model is `source-linked cards/excerpts` that can be sorted, grouped, linked, and promoted.

- Top summary-board pattern worth borrowing: every captured item should carry its provenance. Text:
Here’s the mock direction I’d use as the working baseline while you’re out.

**1. Product Map**
The product should stop feeling like disconnected pages and start feeling like one persistent workspace.

```text
Master System
  Obsidian vault
  Anki
  Brain / long-term outputs

Projects Dashboard
  New Project
  Recent Projects
  Saved Projects
  Active Sessions

Project: Anatomy
  Overview
  Sessions
  Studio
  Tutor
  Publish

Session: Week 5 Thorax PPT
  Materials
  Tutor chat
  Session notes
  Studio Inbox
  Summary Board
  Timer
  Voice capture
```

Core rule:
- once you open a `Project`, the app stays in that project context until you close it
- switching between `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Sessions`, and `Publish` should not reload or forget your working state

Core hierarchy:
- `Master -> Project -> Session`
- `Session -> Studio Inbox / Summary Board -> Project -> Master`

**2. How It Works**
1. Open `Projects Dashboard`.
2. Start or resume a `Project` like `Anatomy`.
3. Inside that project, start a `Session` like `Week 5 Thorax PPT`.
4. Use `Studio` for file viewing, prep, sorting, objectives, maps, tables, and cleanup.
5. Use `Tutor` for the actual studying, explanation, questioning, and main sense-making.
6. While in Tutor, send important content to Studio with:
   - `To Studio -> Note`
   - `To Studio -> Summary Board`
7. If the session gets long, Tutor can compact important material into `Summary Board` so context is preserved and useful pieces are not lost.
8. After studying, return to `Studio` to clean, merge, organize, and turn captures into usable notes/cards/maps.
9. Promote good outputs from `Session -> Project`.
10. Publish finalized outputs from `Project -> Master`.

What lives where:
- `Session`
  - rough notes, temporary captures, Tutor thread, active board items, timer state
- `Project`
  - reusable notes, important maps, approved cards, topic structure, session history
- `Master`
  - final Obsidian notes, final Anki cards, long-term knowledge

│               │                                          │            │

`G. Publish / Promote`

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Publish Center: Anatomy                                              │

│ Session Outputs      │ Project Resources    │ Master Destinations    │
│ Week 5 Thorax        │ Anatomy Notes        │ Obsidian Vault         │
│ - note draft         │ Anatomy Maps         │ Anki                   │
│ - summary cluster    │ Approved Cards       │ Brain                  │
│ - card candidates    │ Session Archive      │                        │
│                      │                      │                        │
│ [Promote to Project] │ [Edit / Merge]       │ [Publish Final]        │
└──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘
```

**4. The Key UX Rules**
- `Project persistence`: switching pages should keep context alive.
- `Session isolation`: rough work stays inside the session by default.
- `Studio Inbox`: this replaces the vague “artifact” idea.
- `Summary Board`: this is where compactions, clusters, and cleanup happen.
- `Viewer + notes at once`: must be possible on one screen and better on two.
- `Timer + mic`: should be always reachable in both Studio and Tutor.
- `Provenance`: anything sent to Studio should keep `project`, `session`, `source file`, and `page/time` when available.

**5. What To Borrow From Other Products**
The best ideas from current tools are consistent:
- [LiquidText](https://www.liquidtext.net/features): `parallel source + workspace`, drag excerpts into a work area, source-linked notes, multi-monitor thinking.
- [Flexcil](https://www.flexcil.com/): `viewer + handwritten notebook` side by side with low-friction pen use.
- [MarginNote 4](https://apps.apple.com/la/app/marginnote-4-ai-notes-mindmap/id1531657269): `project container`, multi-window, mind map/outliner/card board, timestamp/source backlinks.
- [Heptabase overview](https://www.goedel.io/p/unleashing-the-mind-heptabase): `card-based summary board` and 3-column desktop structure.

The strongest pattern to copy is:
- `open source -> capture excerpt/idea -> send to board/note -> organize -> summarize -> promote`

**6. What Actually Works For Studying**
The Studio should support the strategies that are actually high value:
- retrieval practice over rereading: [Roediger & Butler](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20951630/)
- spaced practice over cramming: [Dunlosky et al.](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1529100612453266)
- concept maps as synthesis, not a replacement for retrieval: [Karpicke & Blunt](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21252317/)
- self-explanation and active generation: [Chi et al.](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9769186/)

That argues for this product split:
- `Tutor` = active understanding and retrieval
- `Studio` = organize, refine, cluster, summarize, convert into notes/cards/maps

**7. Which UI Tool To Use**
My recommendation:
- `Best overall`: [Figma](https://www.figma.com/wireframe-tool/) + [FigJam](https://www.figma.com/figjam/)
  - best if you want rough layout first, then real UI planning later
  - strongest for multi-page flows and eventual dev handoff
  - best fit if this will keep evolving
- `Best fast wireframing alternative`: [Whimsical](https://whimsical.com/wireframes)
  - very fast for layout + flow mapping in one place
  - good chance this is the “other tool” you used before
- `Best open-source fallback`: [Penpot](https://penpot.app/)
  - better current choice than Pencil if you want open-source and collaboration
- `Use only if you specifically want old-school offline wireframes`: [Pencil Project](https://pencil.evolus.vn/)
- `Good for forced lo-fi`: [Balsamiq](https://balsamiq.com/wireframes/)
- `Good for whiteboard thinking, not primary UI design`: [Excalidraw](https://excalidraw.com/)

My take:
- if you want to think and sketch fast: `FigJam` or `Whimsical`
- if you want the best long-term UI workflow: `Figma`
- if you want open-source: `Penpot`
- I would not choose `Pencil Project` first in 2026 unless offline-only matters a lot

**8. Questions For You When You’re Back**
- Should `Studio Inbox` be separate for each session, or should the Studio be able to show a merged project-wide inbox?
- When you promote something from `Session -> Project`, should it move or copy?
- Should `Summary Board` be one board per session, or one continuous board for the whole project?
- Do you want the `Publish` page to be a dedicated page or just a right-side drawer in Studio?
- For voice, should v1 be `dictation into current note` only, or do you also want stored voice memos?
- Should Tutor compaction to `Summary Board` be manual only, or can it also trigger automatically when context gets long?
- Do you want `Library` to stay a top-level page, or should project materials mainly live inside the project shell?
- In v1, which outputs matter most to track explicitly: `notes`, `maps`, `cards`, `tables`, `board items`, `transcripts`?

Recommended next step: when you’re back, we should lock the `Project / Session / Studio Inbox` rules first. Once those are fixed, the actual page layout decisions get much easier.

- `Good for whiteboard thinking, not primary UI design`: [Excalidraw](https://excalidraw.com/)

My take:
- if you want to think and sketch fast: `FigJam` or `Whimsical`
- if you want the best long-term UI workflow: `Figma`
- if you want open-source: `Penpot`
- I would not choose `Pencil Project` first in 2026 unless offline-only matters a lot

**8. Questions For You When You’re Back**
- Should `Studio Inbox` be separate for each session, or should the Studio be able to show a merged project-wide inbox?
- When you promote something from `Session -> Project`, should it move or copy?
- Should `Summary Board` be one board per session, or one continuous board for the whole project?
- Do you want the `Publish` page to be a dedicated page or just a right-side drawer in Studio?
- For voice, should v1 be `dictation into current note` only, or do you also want stored voice memos?
- Should Tutor compaction to `Summary Board` be manual only, or can it also trigger automatically when context gets long?
- Do you want `Library` to stay a top-level page, or should project materials mainly live inside the project shell?
- In v1, which outputs matter most to track explicitly: `notes`, `maps`, `cards`, `tables`, `board items`, `transcripts`?

Recommended next step: when you’re back, we should lock the `Project / Session / Studio Inbox` rules first. Once those are fixed, the actual page layout decisions get much easier.

Good. This tightens the model a lot.

**Updated Model**
- `Library` stays outside the project for now as the global materials home.
- `Project` becomes the class/topic workspace.
- `Session` becomes the focused study run inside the project.
- `Studio` is the cleanup and organization space inside the project.
- `Tutor` is still the live study/sense-making space inside the session.

The structure now looks like:

```text
Library
  all materials

Project: Anatomy
  Overview
  Sessions
  Studio
  Tutor
  Schedule
  Publish

Session: Week 5 Thorax PPT
  Tutor
  Session Note
  Studio Inbox
  Session Summary Board
  Timer
```

**What Goes Into The Studio Inbox**
The `Studio Inbox` should only hold things that still need processing. Not final notes. Not permanent project resources.

It should include:
- Tutor captures sent with `To Studio -> Note`
- Tutor captures sent with `To Studio -> Summary Board`
- automatic compactions from long Tutor sessions
- clipped source excerpts from PDFs, docs, videos, slides, and images
- draft outputs made during the session:
  - note drafts
  - map drafts
  - table drafts
  - flashcard candidates
- important open questions or follow-up ideas worth revisiting
- session-level summaries the LLM creates while studying

It should **not** include by default:
- every Tutor message
- finished project notes
- final published vault notes
- calendar items and due dates

Those schedule items should go somewhere else.

**New Piece: Schedule**
Your syllabus / due date / calendar idea should become a separate project area:

- `Project Schedule`
  - syllabus upload
  - due date extraction
  - exam date extraction
  - reading schedule / lecture schedule
  - manual add/edit
  - `Ship to Calendar`

That keeps the Studio focused on study work, while schedule/planning lives in a cleaner place.

So the project gets one more surface:
- `Schedule`
  - review extracted dates
  - approve/edit them
  - push selected items to calendar

**Board Structure**
Your answer on boards makes sense. I’d model it like this:

- `Session Summary Board`
  - only this session’s captures and compactions
- `Project Summary Board`
  - promoted items from multiple sessions in the same class/topic
- `Overall Summary Board`
  - top-level rollup across projects, probably closer to Brain/Master level

That gives you local, project, and global views without mixing everything together too early.

**Recommendations I’d Use As Defaults**
- `Promote by copy`, not move
  - keep the original in the session for history
  - copy the cleaned version upward to project
  - this is the safer default
- `Publish` should be a full page, not just a drawer
  - you have enough complexity now that a drawer will get cramped
- `Voice` should dictate into the current note only
  - simple and fast
- `Auto-compaction` should be enabled
  - compact to `Summary Board` at useful checkpoints, not only manually

**Auto-Compaction Triggers**
I’d use these:
- after a long Tutor exchange
- when token/context pressure gets high
- at the end of a method block
- at the end of a session
- manually if you hit `To Studio -> Summary Board`

That way you don’t lose good material just because the session got long.

**What Feels Locked Now**
- project-based workflow
- session inside project
- Studio as cleanup/organization space
- Tutor as main sense-making/study space
- `To Studio` dropdown with:
  - `Note`
  - `Summary Board`
- Library stays outside project for now
- Schedule/syllabus/calendar should live inside the project
- session/project/overall board hierarchy
- voice into current note only
- auto-compaction is preferred

**Main Open Decisions Left**
- whether promotion should always `copy`, or sometimes allow `move`
- whether schedule belongs as its own top tab or inside Studio
- what the `Project Overview` page should show first
- what output types should be first-class in v1 beyond notes and source material

My recommendation:
- assume `copy-up promotion`
- make `Schedule` its own tab inside the project
- keep v1 centered on `materials + notes + summary boards + Tutor captures`

When you’re ready, I can turn this into:
1. a revised page map
2. updated ASCII layouts including the new `Schedule` tab
3. a concrete workflow from `syllabus/material import -> session study -> studio cleanup -> publish`

# Plan: Course-Backed Project Shell + Studio/Tutor Workflow

## Goal
- Ship a course-backed project workflow where Brain remains the home route, Tutor becomes the project/session shell, Studio lives inside that shell, sessions reuse `tutor_sessions`, captures flow into an Inbox + Summary Board, schedules ship to Calendar, approved resources publish to Obsidian/Anki/Brain, and v1 includes viewer/note popouts plus note dictation.

## Constraints
- Brain stays the canonical landing route and project launch surface.
- Tutor stays the primary live study route and owns active workspace tools.
- Studio is not a new top-level route in v1; it is a Tutor-shell mode.
- Projects are course-backed only in v1.
- Library stays a top-level support page.
- Promotion must ask `copy` vs `move` every time.
- Popout scope is limited to `viewer` and `current note editor` in v1.
- Voice scope is limited to browser dictation into the current note; no audio memo storage.
- Existing Tutor, Calendar, syllabus, vault, and video contracts must remain backward-compatible through rollout.

## Out of scope
- Freeform non-course projects.
- A standalone public `/studio` route.
- iPad live sync / collaborative cross-device editing.
- Full conversational voice control.
- High-fidelity PPTX viewer parity in the first wave.
- Replacing Library or Calendar as top-level support routes.

## Assumptions
- `Project = existing course`; no new project table in v1.
- `Session = existing tutor_session`; no second session system is introduced.
- Archived/inactive courses remain openable as read-only projects; no course reassignment in v1.
- Every capture lands in `Studio Inbox` first; `Summary Board` references captures instead of owning duplicate content.
- Publish acts only on `project-promoted resources`, not raw session captures.
- Brain home adds a `Projects Dashboard` section rather than redirecting the app away from Brain.
- Session start prompts for `Studio` or `Tutor` and remembers the last choice.
- Calendar keeps its top-level route and gains aggregate filters sourced from project schedules.
- This version already incorporates multi-agent gap review: launch-path clarity, Studio/Tutor separation, project lifecycle rules, Inbox/Board rules, publish contract, migration safety, feature flags, telemetry, and full-circle smoke gates.

## Orchestration strategy
- Mode: `swarm-waves`
- Why this mode:
  - The data contract and Tutor shell must be built in sequence to avoid drift.
  - Once the contract is frozen, some frontend and backend tasks can move in parallel without shared-file conflicts.
  - The final hardening wave needs centralized integration because state, restore, publish, and popouts cross subsystem boundaries.

  - `studio_boards(id, course_id, tutor_session_id, scope, name, layout_json, filters_json, created_at, updated_at)`
- New backend API families:
  - project workspace/session launch APIs under a new Tutor-project namespace
  - Studio item/board/promote/compact APIs under a new Tutor-studio namespace
  - schedule aggregation/shadow-publish/telemetry flag APIs layered onto existing schedule/calendar contracts
- Frontend shell additions:

  - Studio surfaces: `Inbox`, `Session Summary Board`, `Project Summary Board`, `Overall Summary Board`

  - Locked decisions from `Constraints`, `Assumptions`, and `Important API / interface additions`

  - Existing proof surfaces: Brain/Tutor/Library/Calendar page tests, Tutor workspace tests, artifact/vault/video/calendar backend tests
  - Required feature flags and rollout gates
  - Required live smoke path: `Brain home -> project launch -> session create -> study turn -> capture -> schedule/publish -> restore -> end -> delete`

## Risks and mitigations
- Risk: New project/session model duplicates existing course/session systems.
  - Mitigation: lock `Project = course` and `Session = tutor_session` in Phase 1; reject any task that introduces a second domain model.
- Risk: Studio inside Tutor shell becomes a confusing pseudo-route.
  - Mitigation: distinct mode headers/actions, single launch path from Brain, and explicit mode state/restore rules.
- Risk: Inbox and Summary Board become redundant clutter.
  - Mitigation: Inbox is the primary queue; boards are derived references only; status transitions are explicit.
- Risk: Publish becomes a dumping ground.
  - Mitigation: publish only acts on promoted project resources with readiness states and partial-failure reporting.
- Risk: Popouts destabilize restore/state.
  - Mitigation: v1 popouts limited to viewer + note editor, same-origin only, BroadcastChannel sync, feature flag rollout.
- Risk: Voice/browser APIs behave inconsistently across machines.
  - Mitigation: feature flag, unsupported-state UX, failure telemetry, no audio storage.
- Risk: Data migration breaks existing Tutor or calendar rows.
  - Mitigation: idempotent migration tests, mixed-version reads, rollback/backfill checks before frontend rollout.
- Risk: Happy-path tests miss real failure cases.
  - Mitigation: add negative coverage for SSE interruption, popout close/reopen, voice disconnects, calendar/provider failures, partial publish, and delete telemetry.

## Worker output contract
- Each worker must return:
  - Summary of what changed
  - Files changed
  - Commands/tests run and results
  - Manual checks completed
  - Feature flags touched
  - Open blockers or contract gaps discovered
- Reject worker output when:
  - it invents new product ownership outside the locked contract
  - it introduces a second project/session/calendar model
  - it changes shared Tutor shell files without targeted regression tests
  - it marks a task done without the listed verification evidence

## Completion criteria
- Brain home launches course-backed projects and active sessions without bypassing Brain.
- Tutor route hosts a stable project/session shell with distinct `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Schedule`, and `Publish` modes.
- Studio supports Inbox-first capture, Summary Boards at session/project/overall scopes, and `Note` / `Summary Board` routing from Tutor.
- Schedule imports syllabus-derived items, supports project-local review, and ships approved dates into Calendar while preserving top-level Calendar value.
- Publish moves only approved project resources into Obsidian/Anki/Brain with explicit readiness and failure handling.
- Viewer popout and current-note popout work in v1, and current-note dictation works behind a safe browser capability gate.
- Regression proof exists for migration safety, API compatibility, restore/state persistence, failure modes, and one full-circle live smoke path on `http://127.0.0.1:5000`.
</proposed_plan>

## Final goal
- Ship a production-ready `course-backed project` workflow where:
  - `Brain` stays the home route and launches projects
  - each `Project` is a thin first-class wrapper around one existing `course`
  - `Tutor` becomes the project/session shell with four modes: `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Schedule`, `Publish`

- This revised plan already incorporates agent gap review. No unresolved gaps remain under the locked v1 constraints below.

## Summary
- Backward-built release chain:
  1. final shipped experience needs stable popouts, publish, schedule, Studio, Tutor shell, and Brain launch
  2. those surfaces require normalized project/studio persistence and explicit launch/restore contracts
  3. those contracts require canon freeze, migration safety, API compatibility, feature flags, telemetry, and live smoke gates
- Repo reuse strategy:
  - keep `Brain` as home
  - keep `Library` top-level
  - keep `course_events` for schedule data
  - keep `tutor_sessions` for teaching sessions
  - add only the thin project/studio persistence needed to support the new product model
- Locked v1 decisions:
  - `Project` is course-backed only
  - `Studio` lives inside Tutor shell, not as a top-level route
  - promotion always asks `Copy` or `Move`
  - `Inbox` is primary queue; `Summary Board` is derived organization layer
  - `Schedule` is a project tab and `Calendar` keeps the cross-project aggregate role
  - `Dictation` is browser-native only in v1; no server transcription pipeline
  - popout scope is `viewer` and `current note` only in v1

## Constraints
- Brain remains the canonical landing route.
- Tutor remains the primary live study route and owns active study execution.
- Studio is not a separate public route in v1.
- Projects are course-backed only in v1; no freeform projects.
- Library remains top-level for global material management.
- Calendar remains top-level for cross-project planning.
- Existing Tutor, Calendar, syllabus, vault, and video APIs must remain backward-compatible for at least one rollout wave.
- Feature flags must gate all new write paths and new UI entry points until hardening is complete.

## Out of scope
- Multi-course or freeform projects
- Stored voice memos or server-side live transcription
- A standalone `/studio` route
- Collaborative editing
- Full PPTX fidelity parity in the first wave
- iPad live sync

## Risks and mitigations
- Risk: thin project wrapper drifts into a second freeform project system.
  - Mitigation: `course_projects` is 1:1 with `courses`; multi-course/freeform projects remain out of scope.
- Risk: shell state and teaching state get mixed.
  - Mitigation: `project_workspace_state` owns shell mode and restore; `tutor_sessions` owns live teaching only.
- Risk: Studio storage regresses into another opaque blob.
  - Mitigation: normalized Studio tables, revisions, board entries, action audit, optimistic versioning.
- Risk: Publish becomes ambiguous.
  - Mitigation: publish acts only on promoted project resources with readiness states and retries.
- Risk: Dictation becomes a backend rabbit hole.
  - Mitigation: v1 is browser-native only, behind capability gate; server transcription deferred.
- Risk: Popouts corrupt state.
  - Mitigation: limited popout scope, same-origin only, edit lease, heartbeat, conflict banner, feature flag.
- Risk: schedule data duplicates across Studio and Calendar.
  - Mitigation: project schedule reads from aggregate APIs over `course_events`; Calendar remains the global rollup surface.
- Risk: rollout breaks current Tutor users.
  - Mitigation: compatibility fixtures, feature flags, shadow mode, and full-circle smoke gate.

## Worker output contract
- Each worker must return:
  - task ID completed
  - summary of changes
  - files changed
  - tests run and results
  - feature flags touched
  - manual checks completed
  - blockers discovered
- Reject worker output when:
  - it introduces freeform projects
  - it stores major Studio state in a single new JSON blob
  - it changes Tutor shell behavior without the listed frontend regressions
  - it marks work complete without the required verification evidence

## Completion criteria
- Brain remains home and launches course-backed projects and active sessions.
- Tutor route hosts a stable project/session shell with distinct `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Schedule`, and `Publish` modes.
- Sessions reuse `tutor_sessions`; shell state is separate and restorable.
- Studio supports Inbox-first capture, Summary Boards, compaction, promotion, and full workspace tool routing.
- Project Schedule imports syllabus-derived data, reviews dates, and ships selected items to Calendar.
- Publish only acts on promoted project resources and supports Obsidian / Anki / Brain with partial-failure handling.
- Viewer and current-note popouts work in v1 without silent state forks.
- Dictation can insert text into the current note when the browser supports it, and degrades safely when it does not.
- Migration safety, API compatibility, failure-path coverage, and one full-circle live smoke path all pass before rollout.

[$trey-autoresearch](C:\\Users\\treyt\\.agents\\skills\\trey-autoresearch\\SKILL.md)to see if we can find any gaps to improve or just overall improvements to this plan we made. # Plan: Course-Backed Project Shell With Studio, Sessions, Schedule, Publish, Popouts, and Dictation

## Final goal
- Ship a production-ready `course-backed project` workflow where:
  - `Brain` stays the home route and launches projects
  - each `Project` is a thin first-class wrapper around one existing `course`
  - `Tutor` becomes the project/session shell with four modes: `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Schedule`, `Publish`
  - each study run is a `Session` backed by existing `tutor_sessions`
  - Tutor can send content to Studio as `Note` or `Summary Board`
  - Studio owns Inbox, Summary Boards, cleanup, promotion, and workbench tools
  - Project Schedule reuses syllabus + calendar infrastructure
  - Publish moves approved project resources to Obsidian / Anki / Brain
  - v1 includes `viewer popout`, `current note popout`, and `browser-native dictation into current note`
- This revised plan already incorporates agent gap review. No unresolved gaps remain under the locked v1 constraints below.

## Summary
- Backward-built release chain:
  1. final shipped experience needs stable popouts, publish, schedule, Studio, Tutor shell, and Brain launch
  2. those surfaces require normalized project/studio persistence and explicit launch/restore contracts
  3. those contracts require canon freeze, migration safety, API compatibility, feature flags, telemetry, and live smoke gates
- Repo reuse strategy:
  - keep `Brain` as home
  - keep `Library` top-level
  - keep `course_events` for schedule data
  - keep `tutor_sessions` for teaching sessions
  - add only the thin project/studio persistence needed to support the new product model
- Locked v1 decisions:
  - `Project` is course-backed only
  - `Studio` lives inside Tutor shell, not as a top-level route
  - promotion always asks `Copy` or `Move`
  - `Inbox` is primary queue; `Summary Board` is derived organization layer
  - `Schedule` is a project tab and `Calendar` keeps the cross-project aggregate role
  - `Dictation` is browser-native only in v1; no server transcription pipeline
  - popout scope is `viewer` and `current note` only in v1

## Constraints
- Brain remains the canonical landing route.
- Tutor remains the primary live study route and owns active study execution.
- Studio is not a separate public route in v1.
- Projects are course-backed only in v1; no freeform projects.
- Library remains top-level for global material management.
- Calendar remains top-level for cross-project planning.
- Existing Tutor, Calendar, syllabus, vault, and video APIs must remain backward-compatible for at least one rollout wave.
- Feature flags must gate all new write paths and new UI entry points until hardening is complete.

## Out of scope
- Multi-course or freeform projects
- Stored voice memos or server-side live transcription
- A standalone `/studio` route
- Collaborative editing
- Full PPTX fidelity parity in the first wave
- iPad live sync

## Important interfaces and persistence changes
- Add thin first-class project wrapper:
  - `course_projects(id, course_id UNIQUE, status, created_at, updated_at, archived_at)`
- Add shell state separate from teaching state:
  - `project_workspace_state(project_id UNIQUE, active_tutor_session_id, last_mode, active_board_scope, active_board_id, viewer_state_json, selected_material_ids_json, revision, updated_at)`
- Normalize Studio persistence:
  - `studio_items(id, project_id, tutor_session_id, scope, item_type, source_kind, title, body_markdown, source_path, source_locator_json, payload_json, status, version, deleted_at, created_at, updated_at)`
  - `studio_item_revisions(id, studio_item_id, revision, body_markdown, payload_json, source_locator_json, created_at)`
  - `studio_boards(id, project_id, tutor_session_id, scope, name, version, deleted_at, created_at, updated_at)`
  - `studio_board_entries(id, board_id, studio_item_id, group_key, column_key, x, y, w, h, sort_order, updated_at)`
  - `studio_actions(id, idempotency_key, project_id, tutor_session_id, action_type, destination_kind, request_id, status, details_json, created_at)`
- Add project read model API:
  - project summary endpoint joining project metadata, active session, pending schedule items, recent studio items, publish state, and continuation state
- Add Studio command APIs:

- Add project schedule aggregate API over existing `course_events`

## Risks and mitigations
- Risk: thin project wrapper drifts into a second freeform project system.
  - Mitigation: `course_projects` is 1:1 with `courses`; multi-course/freeform projects remain out of scope.
- Risk: shell state and teaching state get mixed.
  - Mitigation: `project_workspace_state` owns shell mode and restore; `tutor_sessions` owns live teaching only.
- Risk: Studio storage regresses into another opaque blob.
  - Mitigation: normalized Studio tables, revisions, board entries, action audit, optimistic versioning.
- Risk: Publish becomes ambiguous.
  - Mitigation: publish acts only on promoted project resources with readiness states and retries.
- Risk: Dictation becomes a backend rabbit hole.
  - Mitigation: v1 is browser-native only, behind capability gate; server transcription deferred.

  - Mitigation: limited popout scope, same-origin only, edit lease, heartbeat, conflict banner, feature flag.
- Risk: schedule data duplicates across Studio and Calendar.
  - Mitigation: project schedule reads from aggregate APIs over `course_events`; Calendar remains the global rollup surface.
- Risk: rollout breaks current Tutor users.
  - Mitigation: compatibility fixtures, feature flags, shadow mode, and full-circle smoke gate.

## Worker output contract
- Each worker must return:
  - task ID completed
  - summary of changes
  - files changed
  - tests run and results
  - feature flags touched
  - manual checks completed
  - blockers discovered
- Reject worker output when:
  - it introduces freeform projects
  - it stores major Studio state in a single new JSON blob
  - it changes Tutor shell behavior without the listed frontend regressions
  - it marks work complete without the required verification evidence

## Completion criteria
- Brain remains home and launches course-backed projects and active sessions.
- Tutor route hosts a stable project/session shell with distinct `Studio`, `Tutor`, `Schedule`, and `Publish` modes.
- Sessions reuse `tutor_sessions`; shell state is separate and restorable.
- Studio supports Inbox-first capture, Summary Boards, compaction, promotion, and full workspace tool routing.
- Project Schedule imports syllabus-derived data, reviews dates, and ships selected items to Calendar.
- Publish only acts on promoted project resources and supports Obsidian / Anki / Brain with partial-failure handling.
- Viewer and current-note popouts work in v1 without silent state forks.
- Dictation can insert text into the current note when the browser supports it, and degrades safely when it does not.
- Migration safety, API compatibility, failure-path coverage, and one full-circle live smoke path all pass before rollout.

  - Mitigation: `project_workspace_state` owns shell mode and restore; `tutor_sessions` owns live teaching only.
- Risk: Studio storage regresses into another opaque blob.
  - Mitigation: normalized Studio tables, revisions, board entries, action audit, optimistic versioning.
- Risk: Publish becomes ambiguous.
  - Mitigation: publish acts only on promoted project resources with readiness states and retries.

- Risk: schedule data duplicates across Studio and Calendar.

- Risk: rollout breaks current Tutor users.

I found five real improvements worth baking into the plan before implementation:

- The repo already treats `course_id` as the primary identity for schedule, planner, and Tutor session data, so a new `course_projects` wrapper adds migration and join overhead without clear v1 value. See [brain/db_setup.py](C:/pt-study-sop/brain/db_setup.py#L614), [brain/db_setup.py](C:/pt-study-sop/brain/db_setup.py#L728), [brain/db_setup.py](C:/pt-study-sop/brain/db_setup.py#L2037), and [api.types.ts](C:/pt-study-sop/dashboard_rebuild/client/src/api.types.ts#L47).

- `BroadcastChannel` is a good v1 sync primitive, but it is same-origin and partition-scoped, so editable popouts need a handshake/fallback rule, not just a generic “sync” promise. Source: [MDN Broadcast Channel API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Broadcast_Channel_API).
- Browser-native dictation needs a narrower support promise. `SpeechRecognition` is still limited-availability, and Chrome-family recognition may be server-backed, so Chromium-first best-effort is the right v1 contract. - PDF.js is a strong fit for PDF viewing, but DOCX should be framed as readability-first via `docx-preview` with plain-text fallback, not Office-fidelity rendering. Sources: [PDF.js](https://mozilla.github.io/pdf.js/) and [docx-preview](https://github.com/VolodymyrBaydalka/docxjs).

# Revised Plan: Course-Backed Tutor Shell With Studio, Schedule, Publish, Popouts, and Dictation

## Summary
Keep the existing `T1-T16` phase order and dependency graph, but make five changes before implementation starts:

- Remove the new `course_projects` wrapper from v1 and treat `Project == existing course_id`.
- Keep `/tutor` as the only public Tutor route and add query-param deep links for `course_id`, `session_id`, `mode`, and board selection.
- Keep the `Overall Summary Board` in v1, but make it an aggregated rollup of promoted course-scoped items only. No global-only Studio items in v1.
- Narrow dictation to Chromium-first best-effort with explicit unsupported-state UX.
- Make the “project summary endpoint” thin and split Studio/Schedule/Publish detail into mode-specific endpoints.

## Key Changes
### Data model and persistence
- Replace `course_projects(...)` with course-keyed state:
  - `project_workspace_state(course_id UNIQUE, active_tutor_session_id, last_mode, active_board_scope, active_board_id, viewer_state_json, selected_material_ids_json, revision, updated_at)`
  - `studio_items(..., course_id NOT NULL, tutor_session_id, scope, ...)`
  - `studio_boards(..., board_scope, owner_course_id NULLABLE only for overall boards, tutor_session_id NULLABLE, ...)`
  - `studio_board_entries(...)` may reference promoted items from multiple courses on the `overall` board, but `studio_items` themselves remain course-scoped.
- Keep a compatibility bridge during rollout for current write channels:
  - `tutor_sessions.artifacts_json`
  - `quick_notes`
  - `card_drafts`
- Do not create independent global Studio items in v1. The Overall Summary Board organizes promoted items that already belong to a course/session.

### APIs, routing, and shell behavior
- Keep the canonical route as `/tutor`.
- Add URL query contract instead of a new route:
  - `course_id`
  - `session_id`
  - `mode=studio|tutor|schedule|publish`
  - `board_scope=session|project|overall`
  - optional `board_id`
- Replace the broad “project summary endpoint” with:
  - a thin shell-summary endpoint for project identity, active session, continuation state, and counts
  - mode-specific detail endpoints for Studio, Schedule, and Publish
- Make restore precedence explicit:
  - query params win
  - persisted shell state is second
  - default fallback is last valid course + last valid mode
- Promotion and publish stay course-scoped even when surfaced from the Overall Summary Board.

### Studio, viewers, popouts, and dictation
- Keep `Session Summary Board`, `Project Summary Board`, and `Overall Summary Board`.
- Define `Overall Summary Board` as editable aggregated rollup, not as a separate global workspace with its own raw items.
- Use `PDF.js` for PDFs.
- Use `docx-preview` for DOCX with plain-text fallback when rendering fails or layout is too degraded.
- Explicitly mark Office-fidelity DOCX/PPTX rendering as out of scope for v1.
- Viewer popout is read-only.
- Current-note popout is editable only after a successful same-origin `BroadcastChannel` handshake; if handshake fails, disable editable note popout and keep editing in the main window.
- Dictation is Chromium-first best-effort only, feature-detected at runtime, with no raw audio storage and a clear unsupported/permission-denied state.

## Test Plan
- Schema/migration:
  - verify all new tables key off `course_id` rather than a new project wrapper
  - verify coexistence and migration safety with `artifacts_json`, `quick_notes`, and `card_drafts`
- Routing/restore:
  - deep-link `/tutor?course_id=...&session_id=...&mode=...`
  - stale/invalid query params fall back safely
  - refresh preserves valid shell state without clobbering Tutor state
- Studio behavior:
  - Overall Summary Board can organize promoted items across courses
  - Overall Summary Board cannot create global-only raw items
  - promotion/publish still operate on source course-scoped resources
- Dictation:
  - Chromium supported path
  - unsupported browser disabled state
  - permission denied / recognition failure path
- Popouts:
  - same-origin channel success path
  - channel unavailable path disables editable note popout
  - viewer popout remains usable even when editable note popout is blocked
- Viewers:
  - PDF success path
  - DOCX render success path
  - DOCX fallback-to-text path
  - MP4 open + timestamped capture path

## Assumptions and defaults
- `Project == course_id` in v1.
- `/tutor` remains the only public Tutor route; deep links use query params rather than new routes.
- `Overall Summary Board` stays in Tutor v1 because that was the chosen product direction, but it is an aggregated rollup only.
- Dictation is assistive input, not a core capability required for release across all browsers.
- The existing phase/task structure stays intact; these changes are replacements/substitutions inside the current plan, not a new dependency graph.
</proposed_plan>
